topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email blinked on the screen, stark white against the dark background of my laptop. *Regretfully, your application for the Sterling Scholarship has not been selected for this cycle.* My fingers hovered over the trackpad, frozen above the keys. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest, like a sudden drop in temperature. I reread the rejection, the carefully chosen words feeling like shards of glass in my throat. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling my hurried footsteps. Each step seemed to amplify the silence of the room, a deafening echo of the decision. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of deflation wash over me.

[story 2]

"Denied." The single word echoed in the cavern of my mind, loud and sharp. I stared at the printed letter on my desk, the official seal looking suddenly mocking. My hands began to tremble, the paper slick beneath my grip. I tried to force a steady breath, but it hitched in my chest, turning into a tight, shallow gasp. I walked back and forth across the floor, my gaze darting from the rejection notice to my reflection in the darkened monitor. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. I picked up a pen, intending to scribble something, but the ink just smeared into an ugly blotch. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating.

[story 3]

I reread the notification three times, my eyes blurring slightly. The carefully constructed edifice of my hopes, built brick by painstaking brick over the last year, seemed to crumble into dust beneath the weight of that single sentence. A strange heat prickled behind my eyes. I pushed my chair back so violently it scraped across the wooden floor. I needed air, needed distance. I walked out onto the campus quad, the midday sun feeling suddenly too bright, too exposing. Every passing student seemed vibrant, engaged in activities I felt utterly disconnected from. I stopped under an oak tree, leaning my forehead against the rough bark, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying instability inside.
